summary: Reviewed requirements.md (R1–R13) for completeness and atomicity against the ask, discovery_notes.md, and open_questions.md. ATOMICITY: every requirement states a single observable behavior with a clear precondition and outcome, and each is testable via system-level checks — default-from-system (R1), undeterminable-system fallback (R2), visible toggle (R3), both switch directions (R4/R5), immediacy without reload (R6), toggle reflects active state (R7), persistence across visits (R8), manual-over-system precedence (R9), live reaction to system changes when in auto (R10) and preservation of the override otherwise (R11), full dark-theme coverage (R12), and color-conveyed meaning staying distinguishable (R13, matching the guide's 'status never by color alone'). R12 enumerates element types but expresses one need ('no area left un-themed'), so it stays singular; R9 partially overlaps R8+R11 but adds no compound requirement. COMPLETENESS: the ask's three needs (dark theme, user toggle, default-to-system) are all covered. The candidate missing needs I probed — the inability to return to 'follow system' once the two-state toggle is used, no-flash/FOUC on first load, coverage of logged-out/auth screens that lack the sidebar, per-device vs. per-account persistence, and a specific contrast target — are each explicitly surfaced in open_questions.md (Q2, Q5, Q3, Q1, Q7) with a stated assumed default. They are deliberately deferred product decisions, not overlooked user needs, so completeness holds for a business-level requirements set. No blocker/major/minor gap rises to requiring rework. Non-blocking observation for downstream phases only: R8/R9 make the system-follow default one-way (lost after the first manual toggle) — a reader of requirements.md alone would not see this limitation, which lives only in open_questions.md Q2; worth confirming with the human if 'default to system settings' is expected to remain reachable.
